lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Why the King of France is so suddenly gone back know you the reason?

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Something he left imperfect in the state, which since his coming forth

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: is thought of; which imports to the kingdom so much fear and

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: danger, that his personal return was most required and necessary.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Who hath he left behind him general?

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: The Marshal of France, Monsieur La Far.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Did your letters pierce the queen to any demonstration of grief?

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Ay, sir; she took them, read them in my presence;

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: And now and then an ample tear trill’d down

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Her delicate cheek: it seem’d she was a queen

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Over her passion; who, most rebel-like,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Sought to be king o’er her.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: O, then it moved her.

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Not to a rage: patience and sorrow strove

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Who should express her goodliest. You have seen

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Sunshine and rain at once: her smiles and tears

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Were like a better way: those happy smilets,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: That play’d on her ripe lip, seem’d not to know

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: What guests were in her eyes; which parted thence,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: As pearls from diamonds dropp’d. In brief,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Sorrow would be a rarity most beloved,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: If all could so become it.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Made she no verbal question?

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: ’Faith, once or twice she heaved the name of “father”

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Pantingly forth, as if it press’d her heart:

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Cried “Sisters! sisters! Shame of ladies! sisters!

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Kent! father! sisters! What, i’ the storm? i’ the night?

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Let pity not be believed!” There she shook

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: The holy water from her heavenly eyes,

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: And clamour moisten’d: then away she started

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: To deal with grief alone.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: It is the stars,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: The stars above us, govern our conditions;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Else one self mate and mate could not beget

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Such different issues. You spoke not with her since?

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: No.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Was this before the king return’d?

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: No, since.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Well, sir, the poor distressed Lear’s i’ the town;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Who sometime, in his better tune, remembers

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: What we are come about, and by no means

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Will yield to see his daughter.

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Why, good sir?

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: A sovereign shame so elbows him: his own unkindness,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: That stripp’d her from his benediction, turn’d her

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: To foreign casualties, gave her dear rights

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: To his dog-hearted daughters, these things sting

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: His mind so venomously, that burning shame

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Detains him from Cordelia.

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: Alack, poor gentleman!

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Of Albany’s and Cornwall’s powers you heard not?

lines.speaker: GENTLEMAN
lines.text: ’Tis so, they are afoot.

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Well, sir, I’ll bring you to our master Lear,

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: And leave you to attend him: some dear cause

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Will in concealment wrap me up awhile;

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: When I am known aright, you shall not grieve

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Lending me this acquaintance. I pray you, go

lines.speaker: KENT
lines.text: Along with me.

glosses.definition: fate — the stars govern character, Kent says
against nature — Kent's understatement for what the sisters have done
unfinished; partial — the French campaign Kent cannot describe in full

annotations.title: 4.3 is largely a Q1-only scene — a second textual crux
annotations.body: The Gentleman's report to Kent about Cordelia receiving the news of her father — her tears and smilets, her face like 'sunshine and rain at once' — is in many modern conflated editions (including Standard Ebooks') but is substantially ABSENT from the 1623 Folio. This is the second major Q1/F1 divergence in the play, alongside 3.6's mock trial. The scene's function is to prepare the audience emotionally for Cordelia's reappearance in 4.4: we see her grief refracted through a witness before we see her directly. The Folio's excision of this scene, if deliberate, would make Cordelia's return more abrupt; modern editorial practice overwhelmingly restores it. Readers encountering the conflated text should know that the Folio editors saw fit to cut it.
sources: Gary Taylor and Michael Warren (eds.), The Division of the Kingdoms (1983)

annotations.title: 'Sunshine and rain at once' — Cordelia's composed grief
annotations.body: The Gentleman's description of Cordelia receiving news of her father — 'You have seen / Sunshine and rain at once; her smiles and tears / Were like, a better way' — is one of Shakespeare's most compressed emotional paintings. 'A better way' qualifies the cliché: her grief is more orderly than natural weather. 'Happy smilets... Those happy smilets, / That play'd on her ripe lip, seem'd not to know / What guests were in her eyes.' The face is doing two things at once, and Shakespeare's language tracks both. The passage is largely Q1-only and one of the reasons modern conflated editions feel emotionally completer than the Folio alone.

options: It is substantially a Q1-only scene; the 1623 Folio largely omits it — the second major Q1/F1 divergence in the play (after 3.6's mock trial)
It was added by an editor in the 18th century
